questions.question: Which among the following schedules deals with allocation of seats in Rajya Sabha to the states?
questions.explanation: Fourth Schedule deals with allocation of seats in the Rajya Sabha to the states and the union territories.

questions.question: The institution of Central Bureau of Investigation was established in which year?
questions.explanation: The Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI) was set up in 1963 by a resolution of the Ministry of Home Affairs. Later, it was transferred to the Ministry of Personnel. The CBI is the main investigating agency of the Central Government. It plays an important role in the prevention of corruption and maintaining integrity in administration. It also provides assistance to the Central Vigilance Commission.

questions.question: The constitution has fixed the maximum strength of Lok Sabha at ______?
questions.explanation: The Lok Sabha's maximum strength is indeed 552, consisting of 530 members from states, 20 from union territories, and 2 nominated members from the Anglo-Indian community. However, the provision for nominating Anglo-Indian members was removed by the 104th Constitutional Amendment Act. Therefore, while the constitutional provision for 552 remains, the actual current strength, excluding the Anglo-Indian seats, is 550, with 530 from states and 20 from union territories.

questions.question: Direct election is conducted to which among the following offices?
questions.explanation: The representatives of states in the Lok Sabha are directly elected by the people from the territorial constituencies in the states. The election is based on the principle of universal adult franchise. Every Indian citizen who is above 18 years of age and who is not disqualified under the provisions of the Constitution or any law is eligible to vote at such election.

questions.question: Persons from which among the fields cannot be nominated as Members of Rajya Sabha?
questions.explanation: The president nominates 12 members to the Rajya Sabha from people who have special knowledge or practical experience in art, literature, science and social service.

questions.question: Who among the following can introduce a Money Bill in parliament?
questions.explanation: Money Bill is considered to be a government bill and can be introduced only by a minister.

questions.question: Which among the following parliamentary devices is an Indian innovation?
questions.explanation: The time gap between the question hour and the agenda is known as zero hour. It is an Indian innovation in the field of parliamentary procedures and has been in existence since 1962.

questions.question: Chief Minister of a state holds the office during the pleasure of ______?
questions.explanation: The term of the Chief Minister is not fixed and he holds office during the pleasure of the governor.

questions.question: The salaries and allowances of the judges of High Court are charged from which among the following funds?
questions.explanation: The salaries and allowances of the judges, the salaries, allowances and pensions of the staff as well as the administrative expenses of a high court are charged on the consolidated fund of the state.

questions.question: Who among the following authority constitutes Finance Commission of India?
questions.explanation: Article 280 of the Constitution of India provides for a Finance Commission as a quasi-judicial body. It is constituted by the president of India every fifth year or at such earlier time as he considers necessary.

questions.question: Which among the following is NOT a constitutional body?
questions.explanation: The National Institution for Transforming India, also called NITI Aayog, was formed via a resolution of the Union Cabinet. So, it is not a constitutional body.

questions.question: Through which among the constitutional amendment act was Union Territories constituted?
questions.explanation: During the British Rule, certain areas were constituted as 'scheduled districts' in 1874. Later, they came to be known as 'chief commissioners provinces'. After independence, they were placed in the category of Part 'C' and Part 'D' states2. In 1956, they were constituted as the 'union territories' by the 7th Constitutional Amendment Act (1956).

questions.question: Which among the following authorities can remove the chairman of State Public Service Commission?
questions.explanation: The chairman and members of a SPSC are appointed by the governor, they can be removed only by the president. He can remove him under the following circumstances: (a) If he is adjudged an insolvent (i.e., has gone bankrupt); or (b) If he engages, during his term of office, in any paid employment outside the duties of his office; or (c) If he is, in the opinion of the president, unfit to continue in office by reason of infirmity of mind or body. In addition to these, the president can also remove the chairman or any other member of SPSC for misbehaviour.

questions.question: Which among the following is NOT a bulwark of the constitution?
questions.explanation: The bulwarks of the democratic system of government in India are Supreme Court, the Election Commission, CAG and the Union Public Service Commission.

questions.question: The third All-India Services was created in which among the following year?
questions.explanation: In 1966, the Indian Forest Service (IFS) was created as the third All-India Service. In 1947, Indian Civil Service (ICS) was replaced by IAS and the Indian Police (IP) was replaced by IPS and were recognised by the Constitution as All-India Services.

questions.question: Which among the following can establish an Inter-State Council?
questions.explanation: Article 263 contemplates the establishment of an Inter-State Council to effect coordination between the states and between Centre and states. Thus, the President can establish such a council if at any time it appears to him that the public interest would be served by its establishment.

questions.question: Who among the following can declare an area to be scheduled?
questions.explanation: The scheduled areas are treated differently from the other areas in the country because they are inhabited by 'aboriginals' who are socially and economically rather backward, and special efforts need to be made to improve their condition. The president is empowered to declare an area to be a scheduled area.

questions.question: Chief Information Commissioner holds office for a maximum term of how many years?
questions.explanation: The Chief Information Commissioner and an Information Commissioner hold office for a term of 5 years or until they attain the age of 65 years, whichever is earlier.

questions.question: The first Municipal Corporation in India was first set up at _______?
questions.explanation: In 1687-88, the first municipal corporation in India was set up at Madras. In 1726, the municipal corporations were set up in Bombay and Calcutta.

questions.question: Speaker of Lok Sabha enjoys the same rank as who among the following?
questions.explanation: Speaker is given a very high position in the order of precedence. He is placed at seventh rank, along with the Chief Justice of India. This means, he/she has a higher rank than all cabinet ministers, except the Prime Minister or Deputy Prime Minister.

questions.question: Article 19 totally comprises of how many fundamental rights?
questions.explanation: Article 19 guarantees to all citizens the six rights. These are: (i) Right to freedom of speech and expression. (ii) Right to assemble peaceably and without arms. (iii) Right to form associations or unions or co-operative societies. (iv) Right to move freely throughout the territory of India. (v) Right to reside and settle in any part of the territory of India. (vi) Right to practice any profession or to carry on any occupation, trade or business.

questions.question: How many national emergencies has been declared so far in India?
questions.explanation: National Emergency has been proclaimed three times so far - in 1962, 1971 and 1975.

questions.question: The system of single citizenship is modelled based on constitution of which country?
questions.explanation: The concept of single citizenship in the Indian Constitution, where every citizen of India is a citizen of the country as a whole and there is no separate state citizenship, is primarily modelled on the system prevalent in the United Kingdom (UK). In the UK, a person is a citizen of the United Kingdom, not separately of England, Scotland. In contrast, countries like the USA, Canada, and Australia follow dual citizenship systems - one for the federation and one for individual states/provinces.

questions.question: Centre is NOT empowered to give directions to the states in which among the following circumstances?
options: protection of the railways within the state
facilities for instruction in the mother-tongue at the primary education
execution of schemes for the welfare of the Scheduled Tribes in the state
questions.explanation: The Centre is empowered to give directions to the states with regard to the exercise of their executive power in the following matters: (i) the construction and maintenance of means of communication (declared to be of national or military importance) by the state; (ii) the measures to be taken for the protection of the railways within the state; (iii) the provision of adequate facilities for instruction in the mother-tongue at the primary stage of education to children belonging to linguistic minority groups in the state; and (iv) the drawing up and execution of the specified schemes for the welfare of the Scheduled Tribes in the state.

questions.question: Which among the article is the last one in Part IV of constitution dealing with directive principles?
questions.explanation: The Directive Principles of State Policy are enumerated in Part IV of the Constitution from Articles 36 to 51.

questions.question: With respect to fundamental rights, supreme court can issue totally how many types of writs?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court (under Article 32) and the high courts (under Article 226) can issue the writs of habeas corpus, mandamus, prohibition, certiorari and quo-warranto for the purpose of enforcing fundamental rights.

questions.question: In case of a deadlock arising in passage of a bill, the President can invoke which of the following parliamentary machinery?
questions.explanation: Joint sitting is an extraordinary machinery provided by the Constitution to resolve a deadlock between the two Houses over the passage of a bill. President invites two houses for a joint sitting.

questions.question: What is the general minimum strength set for state legislative assemblies?
questions.explanation: The legislative assembly consists of representatives directly elected by the people on the basis of universal adult franchise. Its maximum strength is fixed at 500 and minimum strength at 60.

questions.question: Which among the following authorities can disqualify a person from being a Member of the legislative house on the grounds of defection?
questions.explanation: Any question regarding disqualification arising out of defection is to be decided by the presiding officer of the House.

questions.question: "Free Symbols" of Election Commission is used for _________?
questions.explanation: Election Commission specifies certain symbols as 'reserved symbols' which are meant for the candidates set up by the recognised parties and others as 'free symbols' which are meant for other candidates and unrecognized political parties.
